planting.note: Collect and sow acorns in fall immediately after they drop, pressing 1 inch deep into moist, well-drained soil; acorns germinate readily in spring without stratification. Transplant seedlings to final location before taproots become too long — typically in the first year.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, calcareous or acidic soil in full sun; chinkapin oak is among the most drought-tolerant native oaks and the only species that thrives naturally on dry limestone outcrops and bluffs — a trait that makes it excellent for rocky, alkaline soils where most oaks fail. The toothed, chestnut-like leaves are distinctive and turn attractive gold and brown in fall. The sweet, small acorns are among the least bitter of any oak, maturing in a single season and consumed immediately by deer, turkeys, squirrels, and wood ducks. The tree adapts to a wide range of soils including limestone-derived clays, shallow rocky hillsides, and deep loams, making it one of the most landscapeable native oaks for challenging sites.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in sandy, well-drained, even infertile soil in full sun; shore juniper tolerates salt spray, seaside conditions, and poor, dry soils, making it excellent for difficult coastal and sandy exposures.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, well-drained to poor soil in full sun; shore juniper is native to the sandy coastal cliffs and beaches of Japan and is exceptionally tolerant of salt spray, wind, drought, and nutrient-poor soils, making it a premier choice for coastal gardens, seaside slopes, and hot, sandy bank stabilization. The needles are notably sharper and more prickly than most prostrate junipers, so plant away from paths. The blue-green foliage often takes on attractive steel-blue tones in winter. The cultivar 'Blue Pacific' is particularly low-growing and dense with blue-green color; 'Emerald Sea' has bright green needles. Unlike many groundcovers, shore juniper is largely free of serious pests and diseases in hot, dry conditions where others struggle.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile to poor soil in full sun; avoid clay soils and any location with standing water, which leads to root rot.

directions: Plant in well-drained, infertile to moderately fertile soil in full sun; creeping juniper is a native North American groundcover that naturally carpets rocky outcrops, dry slopes, and sandy barrens from Newfoundland to British Columbia and south to New England. It is extremely cold-hardy and drought-tolerant once established, requiring virtually no care on appropriate sites. Many cultivars have been selected for varying habits and needle colors — 'Wiltonii' (Blue Rug) hugs the ground at just 4 inches tall and spreads 6–8 feet; 'Bar Harbor' is denser and turns plum-purple in winter; 'Prince of Wales' has bright green needles. The steel-blue berry-like cones are consumed by juniper hairstreak butterfly caterpillars and numerous birds, especially waxwings and thrushes.

planting.note: Plant dormant hardwood cuttings 12–24 in long by pushing them 6–8 in into moist streambank or waterside soil in early spring; roots emerge within weeks and growth is rapid. Seeds lose viability within days of ripening and must be sown immediately.

directions: Plant in wet to moist soil in full sun near water features, ponds, streams, or drainage swales; crack willow gets its name from the brittleness of its branches, which snap off at the joints in storms — a trait that is actually part of its natural dispersal strategy, as fallen branches root wherever they land in moist soil. A native of Europe and western Asia now widely naturalized in North America, it grows extremely fast (5–8 ft per year when young) and provides rapid screening and streambank stabilization. The lance-shaped, glossy green leaves appear very early in spring and provide important early-season forage for willow aphids and the beneficial insects that prey on them. Keep well away from septic systems and water pipes, as the roots are highly invasive toward water sources.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings 12–18 in long in moist, wet soil near water in early spring; cuttings root readily. Maintain consistent soil moisture during establishment in the first season.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile soil in full sun along streams, pond banks, or low-lying wet areas; white willow is a large, fast-growing Eurasian tree widely naturalized in North America and historically among the most important medicinal plants in Europe, as the bark contains salicin — the precursor to aspirin. The undersides of the leaves are covered in fine white hairs that give the canopy a silvery shimmer in wind, making it highly ornamental near water. The cultivar 'Tristis' (golden weeping willow) is one of the most familiar weeping trees in temperate horticulture. Like all willows, it has extremely invasive roots that seek water and must be planted well away from foundations, septic systems, and underground pipes.
